description: Four decks of everyday general knowledge: cognitive biases, logical fallacies, music-theory basics and chess basics. Each is answerable from the orientation primer below.
primer:
## How this set works
Four short decks of general knowledge. Each card's answer is covered by the matching section below. Read the section for the deck you're studying.

## Cognitive biases
Systematic errors in judgement:
- **Confirmation bias** — favouring evidence that fits your belief. **Anchoring** — over-relying on the first number seen. **Availability heuristic** — easy-to-recall events feel more likely. **Hindsight bias** — 'I knew it all along'. **Recency bias** — overweighting the latest events. **Negativity bias** — bad sticks harder than good.
- **Dunning–Kruger** — the unskilled overrate themselves, experts underrate. **Self-serving bias** — credit successes, blame failures elsewhere. **Fundamental attribution error** — others' acts blamed on character, yours on circumstance. **Halo effect** — one good trait colours all judgement. **In-group bias** — favouring 'us'. **Bandwagon effect** — believing what the crowd does. **Optimism bias** — expecting good outcomes for yourself. **Overconfidence effect** — certainty exceeding accuracy.
- **Sunk cost fallacy** — continuing because of money already spent. **Loss aversion** — losses hurt more than equal gains. **Framing effect** — wording changes the reaction. **Survivorship bias** — looking only at the survivors/winners. **Gambler's fallacy** — thinking past chance outcomes make others 'due'. **Status quo bias** — preferring no change. **Base rate neglect** — ignoring overall prevalence. **Planning fallacy** — underestimating time to finish.

## Logical fallacies
Errors of reasoning:
- *Relevance/distraction:* **ad hominem** (attack the person), **straw man** (refute a distorted version), **appeal to authority** (true because an expert says so), **appeal to popularity / bandwagon** (true because many believe/do it), **red herring** (irrelevant distraction), **tu quoque** ('you do it too'), **appeal to emotion** (feelings over evidence).
- *Faulty structure:* **slippery slope** (one step must lead to disaster), **false dilemma** (only two options offered), **circular reasoning / begging the question** (conclusion assumed in the premise), **appeal to ignorance** (unproven false treated as true), **no true Scotsman** (redefining the category to dodge a counterexample), **equivocation** (shifting a word's meaning), **affirming the consequent** (if P→Q, Q, so P — invalid), **denying the antecedent** (if P→Q, not P, so not Q — invalid).
- *Causal/statistical:* **post hoc ergo propter hoc** (after this, so because of this), **cum hoc** (correlation taken as causation), **hasty generalisation** (sweeping conclusion from a tiny sample).

## Music theory basics
Intervals are measured in **semitones** (half steps = adjacent piano keys); a **whole tone** = 2 semitones. Key intervals: **major third = 4** (C–E), **minor third = 3** (A–C), **perfect fourth = 5** (C–F), **perfect fifth = 7** (C–G), **tritone = 6** (half octave), **octave = 12** (frequency doubles).
Triads: a **major triad** stacks a major third then a minor third above the root (C–E–G); a **minor triad** stacks a minor third then a major third (A–C–E). Only the middle note (third) differs between them; root and perfect fifth are the same.
The **circle of fifths** orders keys by perfect fifths (C, G, D, A...); each clockwise step adds one sharp, each counter-clockwise step (by fourths) adds one flat. C major has no sharps/flats; G major has one sharp (F#). Sharps are added in the fixed order **F C G D A E B**. A **key signature** is the sharps/flats fixing the key for the whole piece. Each major key has a **relative minor** a minor third (3 semitones) below, sharing its signature (C major ↔ A minor).
Note durations: a **whole note = 4 beats** in 4/4, **half = 2**, **quarter = 1**, **eighth = ½**, **sixteenth = ¼** — each value half the previous. A **dot** adds half the note's value (dotted half = 3 beats).

## Chess basics
Relative **piece values**: pawn 1, knight 3, bishop 3, rook 5, queen 9; the king is priceless. Bishops favour open positions, knights closed ones. Giving a rook (5) for a minor piece (3) is 'losing the exchange'.
**Opening principles:** control the centre, develop knights and bishops, castle early. By idea: **Italian Game** (1.e4 e5 2.Nf3 Nc6 3.Bc4, bishop eyes f7); **Ruy López** (3.Bb5, pressure on the c6 knight); **Sicilian Defence** (1.e4 c5, asymmetrical counterplay); **Queen's Gambit** (1.d4 d5 2.c4, offer the c-pawn to seize the centre).
**Tactics/terms:** **fork** (one piece attacks two), **pin** (a piece can't move without exposing a bigger one behind), **skewer** (front big piece moves, win the one behind), **discovered attack** (move one piece to unveil another's attack), **zwischenzug** (an in-between forcing move), **zugzwang** (every legal move worsens your position, but you must move).
**Special rules:** **en passant** (capture a two-square-advancing pawn as if it moved one, next move only); **castling** (king and rook move together — neither has moved, clear path, king not in check and not passing through/landing on an attacked square); **promotion** (a pawn reaching the far rank becomes a queen/rook/bishop/knight, usually a queen).
**Mate patterns:** **back-rank mate** (rook/queen on the back rank, king trapped behind its own pawns); **Scholar's mate** (fast four-move mate on f7 with queen and bishop).

Every card's answer is in one of these sections.
